title: Emerging Professional
description: You're in the foundation-building phase — learning how buildings actually get made, not just drawn.
personalNote: Don't optimize for title yet. Optimize for mentors and project types.

title: Intern Architect / Architectural Staff
description: You're deep in the production of real buildings — coordinating, detailing, and translating design intent into something contractors can build.
personalNote: The best PAs I know spent years being the most dependable person in the room.

title: Project Designer (Unlicensed)
description: You're the design brain on a project — pushing the concept and making key visual decisions without necessarily holding the license.
personalNote: This is where most people discover whether they want to stay design-focused or move toward management.

title: Design Architect
description: You're the design voice for significant projects — responsible for concept, form, and the ideas that give a building its identity.
personalNote: The best design architects treat every project as an argument worth making.

title: Senior Design Architect
description: You're shaping the creative culture of a firm, mentoring designers, and setting the standards for what great work looks like.
personalNote: At this level, your job isn't to have the best ideas — it's to build an environment where they can happen.

title: Project Architect
description: You're the technical and coordination backbone of a project — making sure the design is buildable, documents are accurate, and the team is moving together.
personalNote: PAs are the people who make everything actually happen.

title: Senior Project Architect
description: You're leading technical direction on complex projects — mentoring junior staff and being the person who's seen this problem before.
personalNote: This is where deep technical knowledge starts becoming organizational power.

title: Construction Administrator
description: You're the architect's presence on the build — reviewing submittals, visiting the site, and making sure what gets built matches what was designed.
personalNote: If you feel most awake on a job site, this is the role that keeps you there.

title: Spec Writer / Architectural Administrator
description: You translate design decisions into precise, enforceable language — the technical specifications that govern what contractors can and cannot do.
personalNote: Spec writers are the least famous and most quietly indispensable people in practice.

title: Project Manager (Architecture Firm)
description: You're the operational core of a project — managing budget, schedule, scope, and client relationships while keeping the design and technical teams aligned.
personalNote: If you naturally track all the moving pieces even when it's not your job, this role is already who you are.

title: Senior Project Manager
description: You run the most complex projects in the office — large budgets, multiple stakeholders, long timelines — while developing the next generation of PMs.
personalNote: Senior PMs are often the backbone of a firm's financial health.

title: Associate / Principal (Titled Roles)
description: You've reached firm leadership — responsible for business development, firm culture, staff growth, and shaping the direction of the practice.
personalNote: Principal-track roles are about building the firm, not just doing the work.

title: Director of Business Development
description: You build the firm's pipeline — identifying opportunities, cultivating relationships, and leading the proposal and interview process.
personalNote: BD directors are often former PMs who discovered they're better at winning work than doing it.

title: Exhibit Designer
description: You design experiences people walk through — museum galleries, branded environments, trade show installations where space, story, and sequence are your tools.
personalNote: If architecture felt too slow and too permanent, exhibit design gives you the spatial thinking at a faster pace.

title: Interior Designer (Commercial)
description: You shape the interior environment of commercial spaces — offices, hospitality, healthcare, retail — balancing aesthetics, function, and client needs.
personalNote: The skill overlap with architecture is huge — this is a direct lateral move that often offers faster client exposure.

title: UX / Product Designer
description: You design how people interact with digital products — mapping user flows, creating interfaces, and solving complex problems through visual systems.
personalNote: Architects who transition here say spatial thinking and systems analysis transfer directly — the medium is different, the mindset is not.

title: Set / Production Designer
description: You create physical environments for film, television, and theater — designing spaces that communicate character, era, and mood.
personalNote: This is architecture's most theatrical cousin — if storytelling through space drew you to the field, this is a real home for that instinct.

title: Graphic / Visual Designer
description: You communicate ideas visually — branding, publication design, wayfinding, digital assets — where typography, color, and layout are your medium.
personalNote: Architecture graduates with strong visual sensibility often underestimate this path — your portfolio transfer is real.

title: Prop Stylist / Art Director
description: You source, arrange, and direct the visual elements in photo and video shoots — creating the environment that tells a brand's story in a single frame.
personalNote: Your architectural eye is a genuine competitive advantage here — most stylists don't have it.

title: Building Code Consultant
description: You navigate the regulatory landscape of building codes, accessibility requirements, and life safety systems — advising project teams on compliance from early design through permitting.
personalNote: Code consultants are one of the most reliably in-demand and underrated roles in the profession.

title: Plans Examiner
description: You review permit applications for code compliance — analyzing drawings and ensuring buildings meet safety standards before a shovel hits the ground.
personalNote: Every building you approve (or flag) shapes the safety of the people who'll use it.

title: BIM Manager / VDC Coordinator
description: You manage the digital building models that coordination teams and contractors rely on — setting standards, training staff, and solving the information problems that derail projects.
personalNote: BIM management is one of the clearest bridges between technical architecture and technology roles.

title: Energy / Sustainability Analyst
description: You model and analyze building performance — energy use, carbon impact, daylighting — and translate that analysis into design decisions that actually get built.
personalNote: If sustainability is a genuine conviction and not just a credential, this role puts you at the center of the profession's most important challenge.

title: Structural / MEP Firm Architect
description: You work embedded in an engineering firm — bringing architectural coordination, spatial reasoning, and document production skills to structural or MEP teams.
personalNote: Engineering firms often pay more and offer better stability — and your ability to read architectural drawings is the competitive advantage.

title: Construction Manager
description: You manage the physical construction process — schedule, budget, subcontractors, safety, and quality — from groundbreaking through closeout.
personalNote: Architects on the CM path often describe it as finally getting to make real decisions.

title: Owner's Representative
description: You represent the building owner throughout the entire development process — managing architects, contractors, and consultants to protect the owner's interests from design through occupancy.
personalNote: Owner's reps sit at the top of the project food chain without having to run a firm.

title: Real Estate Development Coordinator
description: You support the development process — site analysis, feasibility, permitting, and financing coordination for developers building new projects.
personalNote: Architects who understand how buildings get financed are rare and valuable.

title: Facilities Manager
description: You manage the ongoing operation, maintenance, and improvement of built environments — ensuring buildings work as intended for the organizations that inhabit them.
personalNote: Facilities roles give architects a direct connection to how buildings actually perform over time — which most firms never see.

title: Capital Projects Manager
description: You manage the owner's portfolio of major capital investments — overseeing multiple concurrent projects, managing designers and contractors, and reporting to executive leadership.
personalNote: Capital project managers inside large institutions often have more real authority than most firm principals.

title: Program Manager (Large Owner)
description: You manage a continuous program of construction for a large institutional or governmental owner — shaping standards and processes across a sustained portfolio of building projects.
personalNote: This is a true leadership role for architects who want organizational scale without running a firm.

title: Real Estate Developer (Small Scale)
description: You identify, finance, design, build, and sell or lease real estate projects — taking on full risk and full reward as the person responsible for the whole idea.
personalNote: Small-scale development is for architects who want to be on the owner's side of the table and are willing to take real financial risk for real financial upside.
